main_ingredients: large green chillies (Bhavnagri or banana peppers)
allergens: gluten (gram flour may contain traces; dish is typically gluten-free but cross-contamination possible)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mirchi pakoda consists of 3 to 4 pieces, weighing approximately 120 g, often served as a snack or starter with chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Pakora (besan-battered deep-fried snack) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Mirchi pakoda is a specific variant of pakora where large green chillies are coated in spiced gram flour batter and deep-fried. ICMR-NIN values for besan pakora (deep-fried gram flour fritter) are used as the primary nutritional anchor and adjusted to account for the higher moisture content of the chilli filling and the typical oil absorption of deep-fried gram flour products. Values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for chickpea flour (besan) and green chilli pepper to validate macro proportions.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chickpea flour-based foods and besan pakora analogues — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mirchi pakoda. The GI of 39 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for chickpea flour (besan) products and gram flour-based snacks, which consistently show GI values in the range 35–45 due to the high protein and fibre content of besan and the presence of resistant starch. Deep-frying further slows starch gelatinisation. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (39 × 37.4) / 100 ≈ 14.6, where carbohydrate per 120 g serving = 31.2 × 1.2 = 37.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) and deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit mirchi pakoda intake due to moderate sodium content (~372 mg per 120 g serving) and the high fat load from deep-frying, which may contribute to weight gain and elevated blood pressure; portion control and infrequent consumption are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load and high fat content from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although mirchi pakoda has a low glycemic index (~39) due to the besan base, the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~14.6) and the high fat content from deep-frying can impair insulin sensitivity; people with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this food occasionally and in small portions, pairing it with a low-GI meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat and energy density from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mirchi pakoda is energy-dense (~298 kcal/100 g) with significant total fat (~16.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food, preferring baked or air-fried alternatives to reduce saturated fat and caloric load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mirchi Pakoda (also called Mirchi Pakora, Chilli Bhajji, or Mirchi Bajji) is a popular Indian deep-fried snack made by coating large green chillies — typically the mild Bhavnagri or banana pepper variety — in a spiced gram flour (besan) batter and frying them until golden and crisp. The exterior is crunchy and savoury, while the interior chilli becomes soft and mildly pungent. The batter is seasoned with cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli powder, and salt, and sometimes the chilli is stuffed with a spiced potato or paneer filling before battering. Mirchi pakoda is a staple street food across India, especially popular in Rajasthan, Andhra Pradesh, and Maharashtra, and is widely served in Indian restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~298 kcal/100 g) due to oil absorption during deep-frying, with moderate protein (~7.8 g/100 g) from besan, significant carbohydrate (~31.2 g/100 g), and notable dietary fibre (~4.1 g/100 g). The gram flour base gives it a low glycemic index (~39), making the glycemic response more gradual than wheat-based snacks. However, the high fat content (~16.5 g/100 g) and moderate sodium (~310 mg/100 g) make it a food to consume in moderation for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and halal. Served hot with green chutney or tamarind sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chilli pakoda?
What is mirchi pakoda made of?
What is the GI of mirchi pakoda?
What is the difference between mirchi pakoda and mirchi bajji?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for mirchi pakoda exists; values are adapted from besan pakora data in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for individual ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for chickpea flour products. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food is compositionally well-understood and the besan pakora analogue is a close match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN besan pakora data; no direct mirchi pakoda entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this product uses vegetable oil for frying and contains no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA ingredient data. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as all ingredients (besan, chilli, vegetable oil, spices) are plant-based. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA sources for this food category. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for besan-based snacks. 7. GI of 39 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for chickpea flour products (GI range 35–45); no direct measurement for mirchi pakoda is available. 8. Glycemic load per serving = (39 × 37.4) / 100 ≈ 14.6, based on 120 g serving size. 9. The food is served at Mr Tandoor – Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 11.50. 10. Allergen note: gram flour (besan) is derived from chickpeas and is naturally gluten-free, but cross-contamination with wheat is possible in restaurant kitchens; is_gluten_free is set to false as a precaution.
